title: PyPI repository credentials should not be disclosed
description: In most cases, trust boundaries are violated when a secret is exposed in a source code repository or an uncontrolled deployment environment. Unintended people who don’t need to know the secret might get access to it. They might then be able to use it to gain unwanted access to associated services or resources.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

checks.message: PyPI repository password detected. PyPI (Python Package Index) credentials including usernames and passwords provide authentication to upload, manage, and publish Python packages to PyPI or private repository indexes. Exposing these credentials enables attackers to upload malicious packages under your account name, delete or modify your published packages, hijack package versions to inject malware, consume your upload quota, damage your reputation as a package maintainer, and potentially compromise systems that install packages from your account. If you're using legacy authentication, immediately rotate your password at https://pypi.org/manage/account/, migrate to API tokens for better security, enable two-factor authentication (2FA) on your account, revoke any exposed credentials, audit all package uploads and modifications for unauthorized changes, consider deleting and re-uploading compromised package versions, and implement secure credential storage using environment variables or dedicated secret management systems. Note: PyPI strongly recommends using API tokens (starting with 'pypi-') instead of username/password authentication.

PyPI repository URL with embedded credentials detected. This URL contains a username and password for authenticating to a PyPI repository index. Exposing repository URLs with embedded credentials allows attackers to upload malicious packages, modify existing packages, delete your published packages, impersonate your account, and compromise the software supply chain for users who install packages from your repositories. Immediately remove hardcoded credentials from URLs, migrate to API token authentication which is more secure and can be easily revoked, enable two-factor authentication on your PyPI account, use environment variables or secret management systems to store credentials separately, audit recent package uploads and repository activity for unauthorized access, rotate all exposed credentials, and update your .pypirc configuration to reference environment variables instead of hardcoding passwords. For PyPI, use API tokens: generate tokens at https://pypi.org/manage/account/token/ and use them as passwords with the username '__token__'.

PyPI repository username and password pair detected. PyPI credentials (username/password combination) provide full authentication to Python Package Index repositories for uploading, managing, and publishing Python packages. Exposing these credentials enables attackers to upload malicious packages under your identity, hijack existing packages by pushing trojan updates, delete your published packages causing service disruption, consume your account quota, damage your reputation as a trusted package maintainer, and compromise the Python package ecosystem supply chain affecting all users who install your packages. Immediately change your PyPI password at https://pypi.org/manage/account/, migrate from legacy username/password authentication to API tokens which are more secure and provide better access control, generate scoped API tokens at https://pypi.org/manage/account/token/ with minimal required permissions, enable two-factor authentication (2FA) on your PyPI account, revoke all exposed credentials, thoroughly audit your package upload history and repository activity for any unauthorized modifications or uploads, update your CI/CD pipelines and .pypirc configuration files to use environment variables instead of hardcoded credentials, implement proper secret management with tools like HashiCorp Vault or AWS Secrets Manager, and consider temporarily unpublishing packages if compromise is suspected until security is verified.
